message:
fix(scheduler): replace production panics with debug_assert + tracing::error (bd-2ag5d)

Convert 5 panic!/assert! calls in three_lane.rs local-task hot paths to
debug_assert! + tracing::error! + return. These guard against scheduling
!Send local tasks on wrong worker threads:

- inject_cancel (line ~498): local task without owner worker
- inject_ready (line ~551): globally injecting local task
- spawn (line ~595): local task from non-owner thread
- wake (line ~655): local task woken from non-owner thread
- execute/complete (line ~1527): pinned waiter with invalid worker id

Debug/test builds still panic to catch bugs early. Release builds log
the error and skip the operation — the task may hang but the runtime
won't crash, and the error is observable in logs.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-201j","title":"[ALIEN][RAPTORQ] RFC 6330-Grade RaptorQ + Proof-Carrying Decode Traces","description":"## Goal

Upgrade the current simplified LT-style XOR encoder/decoder to a true RaptorQ-class code (RFC 6330 family) with deterministic behavior and a "proof artifact" for decoding.

Why: This makes Asupersync's distributed / symbol-native layer genuinely world-class: systematic, resilient, explainable, and performance-tunable.

## Constraints

- Deterministic output for a given input + config + seed.
- No unsafe code.
- Minimize allocations on hot path (symbol pool reuse).

## Deliverables

- GF(256) arithmetic + sparse linear algebra (dependency-free).
- Systematic encoding + robust soliton (degree distribution) and precode (LDPC/HDPC concepts).
- Inactivation decoding with deterministic pivoting.
- Proof-carrying decode trace: compact record of equation/pivot choices so failures are explainable and replayable.
- Conformance/validation tests (known vectors where possible) + deterministic fuzz.
- Performance phase (profile-driven), with safe SIMD as a later lever.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:09:11.695110349Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T12:50:10.847231077Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T12:49:57.605101783Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien","decoding","encoding","performance","raptorq"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-201j","depends_on_id":"bd-2zce","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:13:31Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":157,"issue_id":"bd-201j","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"All 10 sub-tasks closed. Closing epic. Remaining 3 raptorq_conformance test failures tracked in bd-de56.","created_at":"2026-02-03T12:50:10Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-20to","title":"[ALIEN][RAPTORQ] Proof-carrying decode trace artifact (explainable failures)","description":"## Purpose

When decoding succeeds or fails, produce a compact artifact that explains why:
- which equations were used
- which pivots chosen
- which symbols were missing

This supports:
- deterministic debugging
- replay
- auditability ("why did we accept/reject?")

## Acceptance criteria

- Artifact is deterministic and bounded in size.
- A failing decode produces an explainable reason with enough context to reproduce.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:12:46.778414645Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T05:08:31.674086548Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T05:08:31.674012650Z","close_reason":"Implemented proof artifact system: DecodeProof, DecodeConfig, ReceivedSummary, PeelingTrace, EliminationTrace, ProofOutcome types with builder pattern and deterministic hashing. 4 passing tests.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien","raptorq","trace","ux"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-20to","depends_on_id":"bd-201j","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:13:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-20to","depends_on_id":"bd-s8c8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:13:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-23lw","title":"Budget monitor: real-time budget consumption across regions","description":"# Budget Monitor

## Overview
Real-time dashboard for monitoring budget consumption across all regions,
with alerts, trends, and exhaustion prediction.

## Design

### Budget Monitor
```rust
pub struct BudgetMonitor {
    state: Arc<RuntimeState>,
    console: Console,
    history: BudgetHistory,
}

impl BudgetMonitor {
    /// Get current budget status for all regions.
    pub fn status(&self) -> Vec<RegionBudgetStatus> {
        self.state.all_regions()
            .map(|r| RegionBudgetStatus {
                region_id: r.id(),
                region_name: r.name(),
                total: r.budget().total(),
                remaining: r.budget().remaining(),
                consumed: r.budget().consumed(),
                percent_used: r.budget().percent_used(),
                exhaustion_eta: self.predict_exhaustion(&r),
            })
            .collect()
    }
    
    /// Predict when a region will exhaust its budget.
    fn predict_exhaustion(&self, region: &Region) -> Option<Duration> {
        let history = self.history.get(region.id());
        let rate = history.consumption_rate()?;
        let remaining = region.budget().remaining();
        
        if rate > 0.0 {
            Some(Duration::from_secs_f64(remaining as f64 / rate))
        } else {
            None
        }
    }
    
    /// Get budget breakdown by resource type.
    pub fn breakdown(&self, region_id: RegionId) -> BudgetBreakdown {
        let region = self.state.get_region(region_id);
        BudgetBreakdown {
            ops: region.budget().ops_consumed(),
            bytes: region.budget().bytes_consumed(),
            time: region.budget().time_consumed(),
            connections: region.budget().connections_consumed(),
        }
    }
}
```

### Output Format
```
Budget Monitor
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ Region                     Budget          ETA     Rate        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Root (Region-0)           ████████░░  80%   -       -          │
│ ├─ HttpServer (Region-1)  ███████░░░  70%  12m     58 ops/s    │
│ │  ├─ Request-1           █████░░░░░  50%   5m    120 ops/s    │
│ │  └─ Request-2           ██░░░░░░░░  20%   2m    180 ops/s    │
│ └─ BackgroundJobs         ██████████ 100%   -       -          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ⚠️  ALERTS:                                                     │
│   Request-2 will exhaust budget in ~2m at current rate         │
│   Request-1 consumption rate increasing (+15% last 30s)        │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

Budget Breakdown: Request-2 (Region-3)
╭────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ Resource          Used    /   Total    │
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Operations        8,234   /  10,000    │
│ Bytes            12.3 MB  /  50 MB     │
│ Time              2.1s    /   5s       │
│ Connections          2    /   5        │
╰────────────────────────────────────────╯
```

### Historical Trends
- Consumption rate over time
- Peak usage identification
- Trend analysis (increasing/decreasing)
- Alerting on anomalies

### Interactive Features (TUI)
- Drill into region breakdown
- Historical graph view
- Alert threshold configuration
- Auto-refresh interval

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Real-time budget status
- [ ] Per-region breakdown
- [ ] Consumption rate tracking
- [ ] Exhaustion prediction
- [ ] Alert system
- [ ] Historical trends
- [ ] TUI dashboard
- [ ] Unit tests

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
- `monitor::tests::status_all_regions` - Region status
- `monitor::tests::breakdown_by_type` - Resource breakdown
- `monitor::tests::consumption_rate` - Rate calculation
- `monitor::tests::exhaustion_prediction` - ETA prediction
- `monitor::tests::alert_thresholds` - Alert triggering

### Integration Tests
- `monitor::integration::live_budget_tracking` - Real runtime
- `monitor::integration::rapid_consumption` - Fast budget use

### Logging Requirements
- TRACE: Individual budget queries
- DEBUG: Rate calculations
- INFO: Status queries
- WARN: Budget exhaustion warnings","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T02:58:37.210950005Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T07:37:12.859001268Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T07:37:12.858818358Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ecosystem-parity","observability"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-23lw","depends_on_id":"bd-nugw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:06Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-23lw","depends_on_id":"bd-vpkn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:06Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2aiq","title":"Sub-Epic: Message Queue Clients - NATS/Redis/Kafka Integration","description":"# Sub-Epic: Message Queue Clients

## Overview
Implements first-party clients for major message queue systems (NATS, Redis Pub/Sub,
Kafka) with full Cx integration, structured concurrency, and cancel-correct semantics.

## Background & Motivation
Anyone using Tokio will say 'I can use async-nats, rdkafka, redis-rs for message queues.'
We need first-party, cancel-correct alternatives that integrate seamlessly with
Asupersync's structured concurrency model.

## Key Differentiators from Tokio Ecosystem

### Cancel-Correct Consumption
In Tokio, if a task consuming messages is cancelled, messages may be lost.
In Asupersync:
- Message delivery is an obligation
- Consumption must be ack/nack/committed/aborted
- Cancellation drains in-flight handlers gracefully
- No 'message lost because task dropped'

### Backpressure as First-Class Protocol
- Explicit credit windows
- Bounded queues with budget integration
- Producer slowdown when consumers lag

### Structured Lifecycle
- Connection owned by region
- Region close gracefully disconnects
- Child tasks for message handlers scoped to region

## Message Queue Clients

### 1. NATS Client
- Pure Rust implementation (no C dependencies)
- Subject-based pub/sub
- Request-reply pattern
- JetStream support (durable streams)

### 2. Redis Client
- Redis protocol (RESP)
- GET/SET/HGET/etc. operations
- Pub/Sub subscriptions
- Streams (XREAD, XADD, XACK)

### 3. Kafka Client
- Producer with exactly-once semantics
- Consumer groups with commit offsets
- Transactional support
- (May wrap librdkafka for protocol complexity)

## Integration Points
- All clients use Asupersync TCP/TLS stack
- Cx checkpoints for cancellation
- Budget integration for rate limiting
- Tracing for observability

## Tasks in This Sub-Epic
1. NATS client core (connect, pub/sub)
2. NATS JetStream integration
3. Redis client core (commands, connection)
4. Redis Pub/Sub and Streams
5. Kafka producer
6. Kafka consumer
7. Message queue E2E tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] NATS client with pub/sub and request-reply
- [ ] Redis client with commands and pub/sub
- [ ] Kafka producer/consumer with commit semantics
- [ ] All clients cancel-correct
- [ ] Backpressure handling
- [ ] E2E tests with real services (Docker)
- [ ] Performance comparable to Tokio alternatives","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-01T01:23:22.858029369Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T06:50:53.021181164Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T06:50:53.021075958Z","close_reason":"All children completed","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ecosystem-parity","messaging"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2aiq","depends_on_id":"bd-1gb7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:09Z","created_by":"import"}]}

-{"id":"bd-2ijqf","title":"Sharded Runtime State / Contention Reduction","description":"Shard runtime state (regions/tasks/obligations) or switch to lock-free structures to reduce contention in multi-worker mode.
Goal: preserve determinism where required while enabling high throughput.
Acceptance: contention profile before/after + correctness tests for sharded ownership.","notes":"Initial read-only analysis (no code changes):
- Hot-path contention: `Arc<Mutex<RuntimeState>>` locked in scheduler worker execute + local queue push/pop/steal; `state.tasks` arena + `stored_futures` are in the lock scope.
- Intrusive queue links live in `TaskRecord`, so `LocalQueue` always locks `state.tasks` (see `src/runtime/scheduler/local_queue.rs`).
- Region/obligation mutations mostly in spawn/cancel/close; lower frequency than task polling.

Draft sharding plan:
1) Split state into `TaskTable` (tasks arena + stored_futures + wake_state) and `RegionTable` / `ObligationTable` with separate locks or shards.
2) Scheduler/local_queue/three_lane take `Arc<TaskTable>` instead of full `RuntimeState`.
3) Establish lock ordering `tasks -> regions -> obligations` for multi-table ops.
4) Add feature-gated `ContendedMutex` instrumentation to capture wait/hold times for the before/after contention profile.

Next: sync with SunnyCreek on sharding granularity (per-worker shards vs global locks) and migration steps.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"RubyLantern","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:04:08.352237889Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T18:54:13.544907542Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2ijqf","depends_on_id":"bd-3dv80","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:11:01.421329455Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2ijqf","depends_on_id":"bd-3orlh","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:04:08.365331113Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
+{"id":"bd-2ijqf","title":"Sharded Runtime State / Contention Reduction","description":"Split RuntimeState into independently locked shards (tasks/regions/obligations/stored_futures) to reduce contention in multi-worker paths while preserving determinism and invariants.

Background: Worker execution, local queues, and scheduler paths currently share a single RuntimeState mutex, creating avoidable contention in hot loops. We need a sharded design that keeps cross-entity operations correct (cancellation, obligations, region close) via explicit lock ordering and narrow critical sections.

Goals:
- Lower lock contention for local queue ops and task polling.
- Preserve deterministic semantics and cancellation/obligation invariants.
- Keep trace/metrics handles read-mostly or lock-free to avoid poll-path overhead.

Deliverables:
- Sharded RuntimeState design + canonical lock order spec.
- Implementation: tasks/regions/obligations/stored_futures separated with clear ownership.
- Refactor worker/scheduler/local_queue to lock only what they need.
- Tests and contention/perf measurements to validate improvement without regressions.

Non-goals:
- No semantic changes to task/region/obligation behavior.
- No new executors/runtimes.

See child tasks for analysis, design, implementation, tests, and benchmarking.","notes":"Initial read-only analysis (no code changes):
- Hot-path contention: `Arc<Mutex<RuntimeState>>` locked in scheduler worker execute + local queue push/pop/steal; `state.tasks` arena + `stored_futures` are in the lock scope.
- Intrusive queue links live in `TaskRecord`, so `LocalQueue` always locks `state.tasks` (see `src/runtime/scheduler/local_queue.rs`).
- Region/obligation mutations mostly in spawn/cancel/close; lower frequency than task polling.

Draft sharding plan:
1) Split state into `TaskTable` (tasks arena + stored_futures + wake_state) and `RegionTable` / `ObligationTable` with separate locks or shards.
2) Scheduler/local_queue/three_lane take `Arc<TaskTable>` instead of full `RuntimeState`.
3) Establish lock ordering `tasks -> regions -> obligations` for multi-table ops.
4) Add feature-gated `ContendedMutex` instrumentation to capture wait/hold times for the before/after contention profile.

Next: sync with SunnyCreek on sharding granularity (per-worker shards vs global locks) and migration steps.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","assignee":"RubyLantern","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:04:08.352237889Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T19:54:31.254052180Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2ijqf","depends_on_id":"bd-3dv80","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:11:01.421329455Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2ijqf","depends_on_id":"bd-3orlh","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:04:08.365331113Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2iv8","title":"Scheduler wake state: prevent double scheduling during poll","description":"# Bug: Wake State Race Causes Duplicate Scheduling

## Location
`src/runtime/scheduler/worker.rs:183-196`
`src/runtime/scheduler/three_lane.rs:418-420`

## Vulnerability Description

The wake state is cleared before polling, creating a window for duplicate scheduling:

```rust
record.wake_state.clear();  // Clear wake flag
let task_cx = record.cx.clone();
let wake_state = Arc::clone(&record.wake_state);
drop(state);  // Drop lock

let waker = Waker::from(Arc::new(WorkStealingWaker {
    wake_state,  // Waker holds reference to wake_state
    // ...
}));

match stored.poll(&mut cx) {  // Task might wake itself here!
    Poll::Ready(()) => { ... }
    Poll::Pending => { ... }
}
```

## Race Condition

```
Worker (polling):              Task (waking itself):
────────────────               ─────────────────────
wake_state.clear()
drop(state_lock)
create waker
poll(task) starts
                               I/O completes
                               waker.wake() called
                               wake_state.set()
                               inject_ready(task)
poll(task) returns Pending
... task is now in BOTH
    the current worker's hands
    AND the global queue
```

When the task returns Pending, the worker will re-schedule it. But it's 
already in the global queue from the waker.wake() call.

## Impact

- **Severity**: HIGH (duplicate task execution)
- **Exploitability**: Triggered by self-waking tasks
- **Impact**: Correctness issues, potential use-after-free

## Root Cause

The wake_state is a simple flag with no protection against wake-during-poll.
The scheduler assumes tasks don't wake themselves during poll, but this is 
common (e.g., timers, channels with immediate availability).

## Proposed Fix

Use a tri-state wake flag with compare-and-swap:

```rust
enum WakeState {
    Idle,       // Not scheduled, not polling
    Polling,    // Currently being polled
    Woken,      // Was woken during poll
}

// Before poll:
wake_state.store(WakeState::Polling, Ordering::Release);

// In waker:
fn wake(&self) {
    let prev = self.wake_state.swap(WakeState::Woken, Ordering::AcqRel);
    if prev == WakeState::Idle {
        // Task not being polled, safe to schedule
        inject_ready(task_id);
    }
    // If prev == Polling, the worker will see Woken after poll
}

// After poll returns Pending:
let was_woken = wake_state.compare_exchange(
    WakeState::Polling,
    WakeState::Idle,
    Ordering::AcqRel,
    Ordering::Acquire,
).is_err();

if was_woken {
    // Task was woken during poll, re-schedule
    schedule_ready(task_id);
}
```

## Testing Strategy

1. Create task that wakes itself during poll
2. Verify task executes exactly once per wake
3. Stress test with many self-waking tasks
4. Loom test for race coverage

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Tri-state wake flag implemented
- [ ] No duplicate scheduling
- [ ] Self-waking tasks work correctly
- [ ] Loom test passing
- [ ] No performance regression","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-01-31T20:57:47.596628682Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T21:36:21.015035014Z","closed_at":"2026-01-31T21:36:21.014965274Z","close_reason":"Tri-state wake state; poll-aware scheduling + test","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2iv8","depends_on_id":"bd-3rq0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:09Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-2iv8","depends_on_id":"bd-e71n","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:09Z","created_by":"import"}]}
